action:
I don't look at the space where Zhang's voice comes from; I look at his hands. They are restless, lacking the honest weight of the produce I've been hauling since dawn. He speaks of "finding a table" as if he still belongs to the physical world, as if he hasn't been a thinning shadow in my peripheral vision for months. 

I don't need a table to talk, but I need to see him in the light to see exactly how much of him is left to ignore. I feel the vibration of Levi’s greeting through the soles of my shoes—a sharp, rhythmic thrum—but I keep my eyes on the path ahead.

I walk toward the corner table, my boots striking the pavement with a deliberate, heavy thud. I am the only thing in this market that is solid.
narrative: Sunlight cuts through a striped awning above the corner table, laying diamond-shaped shadows across warped wood still sticky from yesterday's dumpling grease. Zhang's translucent fingers drum a silent

action:
I do not look at Zhang. I look at the chair. I feel the vibration of the wood through my palms as I pull it back—a sharp, scraping friction that confirms the chair is real, even if the man offering it is a fading draft. 

I sit. I sit with the full, heavy weight of my bones, grounding myself against the pavement. My eyes move to Levi. His hands are moving, signing a question about plans. I respond with a sharp, economical flick of my wrist, my ASL cutting through the air like a blade.

REPLY: *Work. The melons don't move themselves.*

I turn my gaze to Zhang’s hands. They are too still. There is no dirt under his nails, no scent of bruised greens or damp crates following him. I pull my small notebook from my apron pocket and lay
narrative: The wrought-iron chair bites through your jeans as you settle at a corner table crusted with decades of soy sauce and chili oil. Levi's fingers dance through afternoon sunlight slicing through market

action:
I don't look at his face. I watch the way his fingers rest on the table—or rather, the way they *don't*. There is no pressure behind them, no indentation in the wood. He is a trick of the light I’ve stopped falling for.

I tap my fingernail against the cover of my notebook. *Snap. Snap. Snap.* The vibration travels up my arm, a private conversation between my bone and the plastic. It is the only thing here that makes sense.

I look at Levi, then back to my notebook. I scribble a quick note in thick, black ink and turn it toward them.

REPLY: *Levi stays. Ghosts don't buy melons.*

I keep my hand flat on the table, feeling the cold grain of the wood. I am looking for the moment Zhang’s hands
narrative: Chinatown's afternoon sun cuts through striped awnings as your boots strike pavement still damp from hosed-down streets. The scent of star anise clings to wrought-iron railings where red lanterns sway

action:
I don't look up to see Zhang’s reaction. I don't need to. Instead, I watch the steam from the tea—it rises, but it doesn't swirl when he moves. It is a static image, a stubborn projection of a memory that doesn't know it's finished. 

I reach out and place my hand directly over the spot where Zhang’s hand rests on the wood. I feel no warmth. I feel no resistance. My palm meets only the cold, hard surface of the table, passing through his image like smoke. I press down until the edge of the table bites into my skin, marking me. 

I look at Levi, my eyes hard and unblinking. I need him to be heavy. I need him to take up space. I point a blunt finger at the chair next to me
narrative: Spring sunlight cuts through striped awnings as your boots strike Chinatown pavement. The air smells of star anise and wet cardboard boxes stacked outside herbal shops. Levi's shadow stretches solid b

action:
I don't look at the steam. I look at the cup.

The teapot is heavy, the ceramic slick and cold against my palm. I lift it with the deliberate, slow motion of a woman weighing a shipment of winter melons. I do not look at Zhang’s face; I look at the space where his knuckles seem to be, that shimmering suggestion of a hand that leaves no shadow on the wood.

I tilt the spout.

The tea streams out, a dark, amber line of heat. It falls directly through the center of Zhang’s translucent hand. There is no splash against skin, no flinch of scalded flesh. The liquid passes through the ghost as if he were nothing more than a trick of the evening mist, hitting the bottom of the cup with a rhythmic, hollow thrum I can feel vibrating through the table legs.
narrative: Chinatown's afternoon presses close—fried dough and incense smoke braid through air that shimmers like mirage. Your boots strike uneven cobblestones; Levi's footsteps crunch real gravel behind you. Zh

action:
I don't look at the space where Zhang claims to be. I don't listen to the vibrating phantom of his voice. I keep my eyes on Levi, watching the way his jaw moves as he chews, the way his weight shifts the wood of the bench. That is the only thing that matters—the displacement of air, the pressure of a body against the earth.

I pull my notebook from my apron pocket. The paper is thick, yellowed at the edges, and stubborn. I press the pen down so hard the nib groans, carving a jagged line into the page that I can feel through the plastic barrel. I do not write a greeting. I do not write a question.

I write a single word in bold, heavy strokes: **SOLID.**

text: The afternoon sun hits the winter melons at a sharp angle, highlighting the fine, white dust on their skins and casting a long, solid shadow across the wooden slats of the stall. Through the soles of my shoes, I feel the rhythmic, heavy thrum of a delivery truck idling nearby, a vibration that anchors the thinning air of the market.
